summary:
The Nguyễn Lords (1558–1777 AD) were the de facto rulers of southern Vietnam during the era of the [[event:2516|Later Lê dynasty's]] fragmentation, controlling the territory from the Gianh River southward and gradually expanding southward to encompass what is now southern Vietnam — lands previously belonging to the Cham kingdom and the Khmer Empire. The Nguyễn lords maintained nominal loyalty to the Lê emperor while exercising complete practical autonomy, building their capital at Phú Xuân (modern Huế) and administering their territories as an independent state. Their rivalry with the [[event:3304|Trịnh lords]] of the north — who controlled the Lê court and the northern territories — created the de facto partition of Vietnam that lasted for most of the 17th and 18th centuries.

The Nguyễn lords' expansion southward — the process Vietnamese historians call Nam tiến (southward advance) — was one of Southeast Asia's most significant demographic movements. The Nguyễn pushed into Cham territories, reducing the Cham kingdom to a tiny remnant; then into Khmer territories in the Mekong Delta, where Vietnamese settlers gradually displaced Cambodian populations from rich rice-growing lands. By the late 18th century, the Nguyễn had extended Vietnamese control to the Gulf of Thailand coast. The Nguyễn lords also managed relations with Japanese merchants through the trading port of Hội An, which became one of Southeast Asia's most important commercial centers. The Nguyễn lords were overthrown by the [[event:2517|Tây Sơn Rebellion]] in 1777, but the surviving Nguyễn prince eventually founded the [[event:3249|Nguyễn dynasty]] (1802) that reunified all of Vietnam.
